title: Bangladeshi Bhuna Khichuri

ingredients.name: polao rice or any short-grain rice, rinsed
visual_checks: Rinse until water runs clear; no need to soak.
ingredients.notes: Rinse until water runs clear; no need to soak.

subs.name: Basmati rice for a more aromatic, separate-grained result — reduce water to 500 ml as basmati absorbs less.

ingredients.name: moong dal, rinsed
visual_checks: Dry-roast the rinsed dal in a dry pan for 2 minutes until lightly golden before using — this step adds a nutty depth.
ingredients.notes: Dry-roast the rinsed dal in a dry pan for 2 minutes until lightly golden before using — this step adds a nutty depth.

subs.name: Masoor dal (red lentils) for a slightly earthier flavour; cooking time remains the same.

ingredients.name: ghee
visual_checks: Essential for flavour; a drizzle added at the end of cooking is key.
ingredients.notes: Essential for flavour; a drizzle added at the end of cooking is key.

subs.name: Equal amount of butter for a similar richness, or extra neutral oil for a dairy-free version.

ingredients.name: garam masala
visual_checks: Added at the end as a finishing touch.
ingredients.notes: Added at the end as a finishing touch.

ingredients.name: water
visual_checks: Approximately 3 cups; adjust to get dry, separate grains.
ingredients.notes: Approximately 3 cups; adjust to get dry, separate grains.

steps.text: Dry-roast the rinsed moong dal in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat, stirring constantly, for 2 minutes until it turns lightly golden and smells nutty. Remove and set aside.

Heat the oil and 1 tbsp ghee in the same pot over medium-high heat. Add the bay leaves, cinnamon stick, bruised cardamom pods, and cloves. Fry for 30 seconds until they splutter and release fragrance.

Add the sliced onions and fry, stirring regularly, for 12–15 minutes until they are deeply golden-brown and have reduced to a quarter of their original volume. Do not rush this step — the fried onion is what makes bhuna khichuri taste distinctly festive.
Add the ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, and red chilli powder. Stir and fry for 2 minutes until the raw smell disappears and the oil begins to separate slightly from the masala.

Add the rinsed rice and roasted moong dal to the pot. Stir well to coat the grains and lentils in the masala. Add salt and 600 ml water. Bring to a strong boil, then reduce heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly, and cook for 15 minutes.

Check that the water has been absorbed and the rice is cooked through. Drizzle the remaining 1 tbsp ghee over the top, sprinkle garam masala, and fold gently without breaking the grains. Cover and rest off the heat for 5 minutes before serving.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: moong-dal → Masoor dal (red lentils) for a slightly earthier flavour; cooking time remains the same.; ghee → Equal amount of butter for a similar richness, or extra neutral oil for a dairy-free version.; rice → Basmati rice for a more aromatic, separate-grained result — reduce water to 500 ml as basmati absorbs less.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook in any thick-bottomed kadai or pressure cooker (2 whistles on medium flame for the covered stage). The technique and ingredients are closely related to North Indian khichdi and pulao — think of it as a hybrid. Ghee is widely available across India. The whole-spice tempering is identical to biryani technique.
